metadata.summary: Implement MCP servers in TypeScript (McpServer + Zod) or Python (FastMCP or low-level mcp.server) with tools, resources, and prompts. Includes templates and implementation checklist.

content.title: MCP Server Implementation — TypeScript and Python
content.summary: Build MCP servers using the official TypeScript SDK (McpServer + Zod) or Python SDK (FastMCP for rapid development, mcp.server for low-level control). Servers expose tools, resources, and prompts. stdio transport is the default; HTTP+SSE is used for multi-client scenarios.

why.p: A correctly structured MCP server works across all MCP-compatible hosts (Claude Desktop, VS Code Copilot, custom Claude SDK agents) without modification. Proper tool descriptions are the model's selection signal — vague descriptions cause wrong tool selection. FastMCP removes JSON-RPC boilerplate and exposes the protocol surface as decorated Python functions, making server development equivalent in complexity to writing a FastAPI endpoint.

list.item: Building a reusable tool server that multiple AI clients will share.
Exposing database, filesystem, or external API access to agents without hardcoded tool code in the agent.
Custom Claude Code agents (~/.claude/agents/) that need domain-specific tools.
Any agent needing database, filesystem, or external API access without hardcoded tool code.
Single-agent prototype with one or two tools — inline Claude SDK tool definitions are simpler.
HTTP transport in environments without an auth layer — stdio trusts the local process by default; HTTP requires bearer token auth.

section.name: typescript-server
section.p: Use McpServer from @modelcontextprotocol/sdk with Zod schemas for type-safe tool definitions. StdioServerTransport is the default for local servers.

example.title: TypeScript: server with tool, resource, and prompt
example.note: Zod schema fields map directly to the JSON Schema inputSchema. Use .describe() on every field — the LLM reads field descriptions to populate arguments correctly.

example.title: TypeScript: complete server with capabilities declared
example.note: Return isError: true for execution errors — the model reads this flag to understand the tool failed and tries to self-correct.

section.name: python-fastmcp-server
section.p: FastMCP is the recommended Python approach: decorator-based, no JSON-RPC boilerplate. Run with mcp.run() for stdio transport by default.

example.title: Python: FastMCP server with tool and resource
Python: FastMCP with structured Pydantic output
example.code:
from fastmcp import FastMCP from pydantic import BaseModel
mcp = FastMCP(name="mcp-server-myproject", version="1.0.0")
class ProcessResult(BaseModel): """Structured result from process_data tool.""" status: str items_processed: int summary: str
@mcp.tool() async def process_data(data: str, format: str = "json") -> ProcessResult: """Process data and return structured result.
Args: data: The data to process format: Output format (json, xml, csv)
Returns: Processing result with status and summary """ return ProcessResult( status="success", items_processed=len(data.split()), summary=f"Processed data in {format} format" )
if __name__ == "__main__": mcp.run()
example.note: FastMCP supports both sync and async tool functions. Pydantic models as return types are serialized automatically.

section.name: python-lowlevel-server
section.p: Use the low-level mcp.server.Server API when you need fine-grained control over the protocol layer.

example.title: Python: low-level Server with list_tools + call_tool

section.name: http-transport
section.p: HTTP+SSE transport allows one server to handle multiple simultaneous clients. stdio is single-client per process.

example.title: TypeScript: HTTP+SSE server with Express
example.note: For HTTP transport, implement bearer token auth — stdio trusts the local process by default.

checklist.check: Choose SDK (TypeScript @modelcontextprotocol/sdk, Python mcp or fastmcp, Go mcp-go).
Choose transport: stdio for local single-client, HTTP+SSE for multi-client or remote.
Declare capabilities matching what the server actually implements (tools, resources, prompts with listChanged/subscribe flags).
Tool schemas: unique name (1-128 chars), clear description, valid inputSchema (type: object), optional outputSchema.
Implement tools/list and tools/call handlers; implement resources/list and resources/read; implement prompts/list and prompts/get.
Return isError: true for tool execution errors; include actionable error messages for LLM self-correction.
Return JSON-RPC error codes for protocol issues: -32600 Invalid Request, -32601 Method not found, -32602 Invalid params, -32603 Internal error, -32002 Resource not found.
Validate all inputs; implement access controls; rate limit tool invocations; sanitize all outputs; log operations for audit; never expose credentials in tool descriptions.
Version the server (FastMCP: version="1.2.0") for client compatibility tracking.
Document all tools with examples; document all resources with URI schemes; document all prompts with argument descriptions.

section.name: typescript-project-template
section.p: Minimal TypeScript project setup for an MCP server:

section.name: python-project-template
section.p: Minimal Python project setup for an MCP server:

example.title: pyproject.toml for Python MCP server
example.code:
[project] name = "mcp-server-myproject" version = "1.0.0" description = "MCP server for myproject" requires-python = ">=3.10" dependencies = [ "mcp>=1.0.0", "pydantic>=2.0.0", ]
[project.optional-dependencies] dev = [ "pytest>=8.0.0", "pytest-asyncio>=0.23.0", ]
[build-system] requires = ["hatchling"] build-backend = "hatchling.build"

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Server implementation best practices extracted from production deployments:

list.item: Use descriptive tool names and docstrings — the model selects tools based on the description; vague names cause wrong tool selection.
Keep tool responses under 10KB; large responses eat context and slow the agent loop.
Log every tool invocation with caller identity, inputs, and outputs for audit trails.
list_changed notifications are not supported by all clients; do not rely on dynamic tool registration in production — restart the server to update tools.
Human-in-loop checkpoint required for any MCP tool that writes state (file writes, DB mutations, API POSTs) — the model cannot undo tool actions.
Tools that return errors must return structured error messages — unhandled exceptions cause the model to hallucinate what happened next.
MCP sampling (server requests LLM completion from client) requires explicit host approval; do not design servers that depend on sampling without testing the approval flow.

example.title: Dockerfile for MCP server (Node.js)
example.code:
FROM node:20-slim
WORKDIR /app
# Install dependencies COPY package*.json ./ RUN npm ci --only=production
# Copy source COPY dist/ ./dist/
# Run as non-root USER node
# MCP servers typically use stdio CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]

example.title: docker-compose.yml for MCP server
example.code:
version: "3.8"
services: mcp-server: build: . stdin_open: true tty: true environment: - NODE_ENV=production - API_KEY=${API_KEY} volumes: - ./data:/app/data:ro
example.note: stdin_open: true and tty: true are required when using stdio transport inside Docker.
